contents: ReviewDo polychlorinated biphenyls cause cancer? A systematic review andmeta-analysis of epidemiological studies on risk of cutaneousmelanoma and non-Hodgkin lymphomaClaudia Zani*, Elisabetta Ceretti, Loredana Covolo, Francesco DonatoDepartment of Medical and Surgical Specialities, Radiological Sciences and Public Health, University of Brescia, Viale Europa 11, 25123, Brescia, Italyh i g h l i g h t s In 2015 IARC upgraded the classification of PCBs to carcinogenic to human. The studies on PCB exposure and risk of cancer provided discrepant evidences. Our results don't support the hypothesis that PCBs cause melanoma and/or NHL.a r t i c l e i n f oArticle history:Received 18 January 2017Received in revised form20 April 2017Accepted 9 May 2017Available online 12 May 2017

Handling Editor: A. GiesKeywords:Polychlorinated biphenylsPCBCancerMelanomaNon-Hodgkin lymphomaReviewMeta-analysisa b s t r a c tIn 2015 a IARC Working Group upgraded the classification of PCBs to Group 1 "Carcinogenic to humans",also on the basis of evidence from epidemiological studies showing an excess risk for melanoma.Increased risks for non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) and breast cancer were also reported though theevidence was limited. However, some recent reviews of studies on PCB exposure and risk of cancerprovided discrepant findings. Therefore, we re-evaluated the association between exposure to PCBs andrisk of melanoma and NHL by a systematic review and meta-analysis. We retrieved 11 independentcohort studies on occupationally exposed workers. About half of them showed increased standardizedmortality or incidence ratios (SMRs or SIRs) for melanoma and none for NHL. The pooled SMRs were 1.32(95% CI: 1.05e1.64) for melanoma and 0.94 (0.73e1.23) for NHL. Among population-based cohort andcase-control studies with individual measures of PCB exposure, one only study was carried out on PCBexposure and melanoma, showing an odds ratio (OR) of 6.0 (2.0e18.2) for the highest compared tolowest quartile of PCB distribution. 13 cohort and case-control studies evaluated the association betweenNHL and PCB concentration in blood or subcutaneous fat, with summary OR  1/4 1.5 (1.1e1.7) for thehighest vs lowest quantile of PCB distribution. However, two cohort studies on people intoxicated by riceoil containing PCBs found no excess of deaths for skin cancer and inconsistent results for NHL. Inconclusion, these findings do not provide a strong evidence that PCB exposure can increase the risk ofmelanoma and NHL in humans.(c) 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) are organic compounds con-taining from one to ten chlorine atoms attached to a biphenyl nu-cleus, including 209 different congeners, largely produced asdielectric fluids in capacitors, transformers and other materialssince the 1930s to the 1980s (ATSDR, 2003). After that date, theirnegative impact on the environment was apparent and subse-quently their production and use were banned in most countries.Due to their long persistence in the environment and their bio-accumulation along the food chain, they have diffused everywhereand have contaminated virtually all human populations. Theirpossible effects on human health are a matter of concern and alarge bulk of epidemiologic and toxicological research is available atpresent.In 2016, the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC)upgraded the classification of the PCBs to Group 1 "Carcinogenic tohumans" from the previous Group 2A classification "Probablycarcinogenic to humans" (IARC, 2016). These compounds wereclassified in Group 1 on the basis of sufficient evidence of carci-nogenicity in humans and experimental animals, and of the evi-dence of an aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR)-mediated mechanismof carcinogenicity for some PCB congeners similar to 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzopara-dioxin (dioxin-like PCBs) in both humansand experimental animals (Lauby-Secretan et al., 2013; IARC, 2016).The IARC Working Group concluded for sufficient evidence of PCBcarcinogenicity for melanoma and limited evidence for non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) and breast cancer. However, a recentmeta-analysis found no evidence of association between PCBexposure and the risk of malignant melanoma (Boffetta et al., 2016).Two reviews on PCB exposure and the risk of NHL found contrastingresults (Golden and Kimbrough, 2009; Freeman and Kohles, 2012).Another review of epidemiological studies on PCB exposure andrisk of cancer showed some evidence for a possible role of PCBexposure in the development of NHL but inconsistent results forother cancers (Zani et al., 2013).Because of these discrepancies between the IARC evaluation andthe results of some reviews, we aimed to re-evaluate the associa-tion between exposure to PCBs and risk of cutaneous melanomaand NHL by a systematic review and meta-analysis.

2. MethodsWe updated our previous search (Zani et al., 2013) consideringalso the studies cited in the IARC Monograph. The methods of thissystematic review matched those described in the PreferredReportingItemsforSystematicReviewsandMeta-Analyses(PRISMA) guidelines.TheliteraturesearchcoveredtheperiodJanuary2013eDecember 2016 and it was carried out using electronic databases ofscientific research (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science).We conducted the bibliographic search using the followingquery strings for each electronic database: PubMed: (((PCB[All

Fields] OR ("polychlorinated biphenyls"[MeSH Terms] OR ("polych-lorinated"[All Fields] AND "biphenyls"[All Fields]) OR "polychlorinatedbiphenyls"[AllFields]))ORorganohalogen[AllFields])AND((("tumours"[All Fields] OR "neoplasms"[MeSH Terms] OR "neo-plasms"[All Fields] OR "tumors"[All Fields]) OR ("neoplasms"[MeSHTerms] OR "neoplasms"[All Fields] OR "cancer"[All Fields])) OR ("neo-plasms"[MeSH Terms] OR "neoplasms"[All Fields] OR "neoplasm"[AllFields]))) AND (((cohort[All Fields] OR case-control[All Fields]) ORfollow-up[All Fields]) OR longitudinal[All Fields]) AND ("2013/01/01"[PDAT]: "3000"[PDAT]) AND english[Language])))Web of Science: (TOPIC:(((((PCB OR polychlorinated biphenyls) ORorganohalogen) OR organochlorine) AND ((tumours OR cancer) ORneoplasm)) AND (((cohort OR case-control) OR follow-up) OR longi-tudinal)) AND LANGUAGE:(English))Scopus: ((PCB OR polychlorinated biphenyls OR organohalogen ORorganochlorine) AND (tumour OR cancer OR neoplasm) AND (cohortOR case-control OR follow-up OR longitudinal))We selected articles according to the following criteria: studiesperformed in humans, written in English, full text available, keyterms present anywhere in the paper, articles with original data.Weconsideredonly a)occupational cohortstudiesand b)population-based cohort or case-control studies with direct mea-sures of exposure to PCBs (in biologic samples) that allowed thecomputation of standardized mortality or morbidity ratios (SMR),relative risks (RR), odds ratios (OR) or other measures of associa-tion. Therefore we excluded studies with only indirect measures ofexposure, such as place of residence, occupational history or PCBmeasurements in environmental matrices, animals or plants.Studies investigating various chemicals, including PCBs, dioxinsor other organochlorine, were considered only if separated mea-sures for PCBs were available. We considered only studies thatshowed data on melanoma and Non Hodgkin Lymphoma.We also included two cohort studies of Asian people undergoingmass poisoning by PCBs because both measures of total intake andof serum concentration of these chemicals were available, thoughnot for each individual. Therefore, these cohorts were consideredseparately from the cohort and case-control studies with individualmeasures of PCBs.Related articles were also searched for, so as the cited papers inthe articles retrieved and in reviews. Two authors (C.Z. and F.D.)examined separately titles, abstracts and each paper included.To avoid duplicate data, we considered only the most recentupdate or the most complete of two or more studies performed onthe same population.Among occupational cohort studies, only those reporting stan-dardized mortality ratio (SMR), proportional mortality ratio (PMR)or standardized incidence ratio (SIR) were used for the meta-analysis, with a summary SMR based on the number of observedand expected deaths or cases in each study, as suggested by Wongand Raabe (1996). Studies reporting no case or death could not beused for calculating the combined SMRs. Studies that provided dataonly for all lymphopoietic malignancies together, including leuke-mias, were excluded, whereas those reporting lymphomas (NHL

and Hodgkin Lymphoma) or lymphoid malignancies (NHL andHodgkin Lymphoma and multiple myeloma) together were used,because NHL are usually the majority of total lymphomas. Similarly,we used mortality data for "skin cancer" when melanoma was notreported separately, because this is the most common cause ofdeath among all skin cancers. Only data on the whole cohorts wereincluded in the meta-analysis. If only separate data for men andwomen were reported, we computed SMRs, with their 95% confi-dence intervals (95% CIs), for total subjects, using the tabulatedvalues of 95% confidence limits limit factors for estimating aPoisson-distributed variable (Breslow and Day, 1987). Subgroupanalyses according to duration of employment, lag-time or cumu-lative exposure were not considered. When more occupationalcohorts were put together, we considered separately the cohort ofworkers at each plant as the study unit.Log-transformed 95% CIs were calculated for estimating thestandard errors (SEs) of the ln (SMR) by the formula: SE of ln(SMR)  1/4 [ln (upper limit) e ln (lower limit)]/2  1.96) (Bradburn,2004). Summary SMRs and 95% CIs were calculated using arandom-effects model (Dersimonian and Laird, 1986). Heteroge-neity of effects across studies was assessed by the Cochran's Qstatistic and was deemed significant when p value was <0.10. Toevaluate the percentage of variation attributable to heterogeneity,we calculated the I2 statistics (Higgins and Thompson, 2002).SubstantialheterogeneitywasdefinedatI2 > 50%and/orpvalue < 0.10 by the Q test.Publication bias was evaluated using the funnel plot methoddisplaying SMRs from individual studies versus their precision (1/standard error) and confirmed using Egger's test method (Eggeret al., 1998). Sensitivity analysis was carried out to evaluate theinfluence of individual studies on the summary effect estimate(Tobias, 1999).A p value < 0.05 at two sided tests was considered statisticallysignificant. Statistical analyses were conducted using STATA 12.0(Stata Corp, College Station, TX), notably the metan commands.

3. ResultsThe present analysis is based on an update of our previousresearch (Zani et al., 2013). Following the Prisma flow diagram(reported in Supplementary material), we retrieved a total of 184studies from January 2013 to December 2016: 90 from Web ofScience, 48 from PubMed and 46 from Scopus. After eliminatingduplicates, we included 121 studies. A total of 21 full text articleswere assessed for eligibility: nine of them reported data on cancer,but only one contained data about NHL and melanoma (Pesatoriet al., 2013). Another article on PCB exposure and melanoma wasretrieved during the revision of the present paper and included inthe review (Kelly et al., 2017).3.1. Occupational cohort studies on exposure to PCBs and cutaneousmelanomaWe retrieved 27 cohort studies on workers in capacitor andtransformer producing factories, in electrical power, and in trans-formermanufactureandtelecommunicationsindustry.Aftereliminating duplicates and separating cohorts for each plant, weincluded 11 studies (De Guire et al., 1992; Tynes et al., 1994;Gustavsson and Hogstedt, 1997; Loomis et al., 1997; Robinsonet al., 1999; Yassi et al., 2003; Mallin et al., 2004; Pesatori et al.,2013; Ruder et al., 2014; three cohorts).In Table 1, we reported the characteristics of these studies, theSMRs or SIRs and their 95% CIs for all cancers and cutaneous mel-anoma. Almost all studies used mortality as a measure of effect, andthe general population of the area or the whole country for

computingtheexpectedevents.Allmeasureswereage-standardized, and separate analyses for men and women wereperformed in some studies. Most studies included workers whohad worked at the factory for at least 90 days. The Illinois cohortwas also exposed to other chemicals, some of which are potentialcarcinogens, such as chlorinated naphthalene and phenoxy herbi-cides (Mallin et al., 2004). For the three US National Institute forOccupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) cohorts of capacitormanufacturing workers (Indiana, Massachusetts and New York),separate data for each plant were used, as described in Methods,which were referred to workers with at least 3 months of workingin the factory, as reported in Ruder et al. (2014).Only two studies reported a statistically significant excess ofdeaths or cases for all cancer (Robinson et al., 1999; Mallin et al.,2004). As regards cutaneous melanoma, four studies showed asignificantly increased risk (Robinson et al., 1999; Yassi et al., 2003;Ruder et al., 2014 in Indiana and New York cohorts). Two studiesfound an increased risk of melanoma of the skin for some sub-groups of workers only: those with lag-time > 20 years and>10000 h of cumulative exposure in the study by Loomis et al.(1997) (RR  1/4 4.8; 95% CI: 1.5e15.1); men, though not women,with lag-time < 20 years in the study by De Guire et al. (1992)(SMR  1/4 9.4; 95% CI: 1.1e34.1). The remaining five studies showedno excess of deaths or cases of skin melanoma compared to thoseexpected (Tynes et al., 1994; Gustavsson and Hogstedt, 1997; Mallinet al., 2004; Pesatori et al., 2013; Ruder et al., 2014; Massachusettscohort).Data on risk of melanoma by duration of working in the factorywas reported in few studies: two of them showed an increased riskwith increasing duration (Tynes et al., 1994; Loomis et al., 1997),whereas the joined analysis of the three NIOSH cohorts did notshow a trend of risk for melanoma with increasing cumulativeexposure, with a relative risk of 0.20 (95% CI: 0.06e0.70) for thehighest level of estimated exposure (Ruder et al., 2014).The meta-analysis of risk of skin melanoma in occupationalcohorts is shown in Fig. 1. All the studies set out in Table 1 wereincluded except one (Gustavsson and Hogstedt, 1997) reporting nocase of melanoma. The pooled SMR, based on a random-effectsmodel, was 1.32 (95% CI: 1.05e1.64; p  1/4 0.03) with a moderateevidence of heterogeneity across studies (I2  1/4 52.5%, p  1/4 0.03). Thefunnel plot displaying the logarithm of the SMR versus its standarderror (figure not shown) and the Egger's test (p  1/4 0.4) showed noevidence of publication bias. The sensitivity analysis showed thatno single study influenced the overall SMR substantially (datanot shown). The inclusion of the pooled data of the three NIOSHcohorts together rather than separately, as reported by Ruder et al.(2014) did not change the results significantly (pooled SMR  1/4 1.17,95% CI: 1.04e1.30; p  1/4 0.008).

3.2. Population-based studies on exposure to PCBs and melanomawith individual measures of exposureOnly one case-control study was retrieved on melanoma andPCB serum levels (Gallagher et al., 2011) (Table 2). A total of 14 PCBcongeners were determined and total PCBs were computed as thesum of single congeners in 80 cases and 310 controls. An increasedrisk of melanoma for the highest compared to the lowest quartile ofPCB distribution was found (OR: 6.0, 95% CI: 2.0e18.2) after con-trolling for the major risk factors for this disease, i.e. sun sensitivityand sun exposure. The association was stronger for total non-dioxin-like PCB levels (OR  1/4 7.0; 95% CI: 2.3e21.4) than for totaldioxin-like PCB levels (OR: 2.8, 95% CI: 1.0e8.0) comparing thehighest versus lowest quartile.A high exposure to PCBs, and polychlorinated dibenzofurans(PCDFs) occurred in people who ingested rice oil contaminated byAuthors, yearLocation ofthe plantFactoryNo. subjectsPeriod of follow-upCancer (No. deaths or cases): SMR (95% CI)De Guire et al., 1992CanadaTelecommunicationsindustry95901976e1983All cancers (n  1/4 84): 0.6 (0.5e0.8)*Melanoma (n  1/4 3): 2.5 (0.5e7.2)*Lymphatic and haematopoietic (males, n  1/4 7): 0.7 (0.3e1.5)Tynes et al., 1994NorwayElectrical power5088 (M)1953e1985All cancers (incidence: n  1/4 486): SIR  1/4 1.0 (0.9e1.1)Melanoma (incidence: n  1/4 19): SIR  1/4 1.1 (0.7e1.8)NHL (incidence: n  1/4 12): SIR  1/4 0.7 (0.4e1.2)Gustavson andHogstedt, 1997SwedenCapacitormanufacture2421965e1991All cancers (incidence: n  1/4 18): SIR  1/4 0.9 (0.5e1.4)Melanoma (incidence: n  1/4 0): SIR  1/4 0 (0e4.7)NHL (incidence: n  1/4 1): SIR  1/4 1.5 (0e8.3)Robinson et al., 1999USAElectrical power30686 (M)1982e1987All cancers (n  1/4 8154): PMR  1/4 1.1 (1.0e1.1)Melanoma (n  1/4 119): PMR  1/4 1.2 (1.0e1.5)

Yassi et al., 2003CanadaTransformermanufacture2222 (M)1969e1995All cancers (incidence: n  1/4 168): SIR  1/4 1.2 (1e1.4)Melanoma (incidence: n  1/4 10): SIR  1/4 2.2 (1.1e4.0)Mallin et al., 2004Illinois, USACapacitormanufacture28851946e2000All cancers (n  1/4 347): 1.1 (1.0e1.2) *Malignant skin cancer (n  1/4 5): 1.0 (0.3e2.3)*NHL (females: n  1/4 13): 1.8 (0.9e3.0)Pesatori et al., 2013ItalyCapacitormanufacture25651950e2006All cancers (n  1/4 183): 1.0 (0.8e1.1)Melanoma (n  1/4 1): 0.5 (0.1e3.5)NHL (n  1/4 8): 1.7 (0.8e3.3)Ruder et al., 2014Indiana, USACapacitormanufacture35801960e2008All cancers (n  1/4 246): 0.8 (0.7e0.9)Melanoma (n  1/4 13): 2.6 (1.4e4.4)NHL (n  1/4 11): 0.9 (0.5e1.7)

Ruder et al., 2014Massachusetts,USACapacitormanufacture125581960e2008All cancers (n  1/4 860): 1.0 (0.9e1.1)Melanoma (n  1/4 8): 0.6 (0.3e1.2)NHL (n  1/4 39): 1.2 (0.9e1.7)Ruder et al., 2014New York, USACapacitormanufacture87271960e2008All cancers (n  1/4 801): 1.0 (0.9e1.1)Melanoma (n  1/4 20): 1.7 (1.0e2.6)NHL (n  1/4 19): 0.6 (0.4e0.9)SMR  1/4 standardized mortality ratio; SIR  1/4 standardized incidence ratio; PMR  1/4 proportionate mortality ratio *computed by the authors.these chemicals in the so-known "Yusho" and "Yucheng" incidents,which occurred in Japan in 1968 and in Taiwan in 1979. The esti-mated intakes of total PCBs and PCDFs were about 1e2 g and 3e4 g,in the episodes, respectively, with an overall daily TEQ intake inYusho poisoning of 154 ng/kg/day, about 10000 times higher thanthe daily intake of the general population (1e19 pg/kg/day)(Masuda, 1996). In the Yucheng patients, the blood levels of totalPCBs were up to 1156 ng/ml, about 1000 times higher than thegeneral population (Hsu et al., 1985, 2005). Several subjects hadacute effects, with dermal lesions typical of dioxin exposure,particularly chloracne, mainly due to dioxin-like PCBs and PCDFs(Hsu et al., 1985).Two cohort studies were carried out including around 1500Yusho and 2000 Yucheng patients, with latest updates at 43 and 30years, respectively (Yoshimura, 2012; Li et al., 2013). No excess ofskin cancer cases or deaths were reported in any cohort, so as in ameta-analysis of the two cohorts (Li et al., 2015) (Table 2).

3.3. Occupational cohort studies on exposure to PCBs and non-Hodgkin lymphomaThe occupational cohort studies included in the melanomaanalysis reported also data on mortality for, or incidence of, NHL(ICD IX: 200-202) except one reporting mortality for all neoplasmsof the lymphatic and hematopoietic system together (De Guireet al., 1992) and another one that did not report deaths from NHL(Robinson et al., 1999). For one of the two Canadian cohorts, weused data from a previous analysis of the cohort (Yassi et al., 1994),instead of the most recent publication (Yassi et al., 2003), whichhowever did not report data on NHL. Therefore, we included 9occupational cohort studies in the meta-analysis of the associationof PCB exposure with NHL. None of them showed statistically sig-nificant increases of NHL risk, and the meta-analysis showed nooverall association between total PCB exposure and NHL (Fig. 2),with a pooled SMR of 0.94 (95% CI: 0.73e1.23) based on a random-effects model, without significant heterogeneity (I2  1/4 43.7%,p  1/4 0.08). Based on the sensitivity analysis, the result for NHLmortality and PCBs exposure was so robust that the exclusion of anystudy did not change the conclusion of the meta-analysis (data notshown). There was no evidence of publication bias based on visualinspection of the funnel plot (figure not shown) in addition toEgger's test (p  1/4 0.3).Analysis by duration of employment was reported in 6 studies:two of them (Tynes et al., 1994; Loomis et al., 1997) showed a trendof increasing risk with increasing duration of employment or cu-mulative exposure, one found the lowest risk for the highest esti-mated cumulative PCB exposure (relative risk  1/4 0.20) (Ruder et al.,2014) and the remaining two reported no data on NHL by duration(Mallin et al., 2004; Pesatori et al., 2013).3.4. Population-based studies on exposure to PCBs and non-Hodgkin lymphoma

In a previous review performed by some of us (Zani et al., 2013),13 studies (7 cohort and 6 case-control studies) which evaluatedPCB concentration in blood or abdominal subcutaneous fat wereselected (Hardell et al., 2001; Quintana et al., 2004; De Roos et al.,2005; Engel et al., 2007; two cohorts; Spinelli et al., 2007; Coccoet al., 2008; Colt et al., 2009; Hardell et al., 2009; Bertrand et al.,2010; Laden et al., 2010; Viel et al., 2011; BrEURauner et al., 2012).One paper reported the findings from three separate cohorts (Engelet al., 2007), but only two of them were included in the analysis(CLUE1 and Janus studies), because an update of the data was re-ported for the third one separately (Laden et al., 2010).An update of our search for articles published since 2013 and acomparison with the IARC Monograph provided one further studyon the risk of B-cell NHL by PCB exposure, consisting in a case-control study nested within two prospective cohorts (Kelly et al.,2017). During 16 years follow-up, 270 incident cases and 270healthy controls were recruited in the study and their pre-diagnostic plasma levels of various organochlorines, including 6PCB congeners were measured (Kelly et al., 2017). This study,however, could not be added to the others included in the meta-analysis because only separate analyses for each PCB congenerare reported. The study found no evidence of an association ofplasma levels of each PCB congener with B-cell NHL, with a para-doxical inverse trend of decreasing risk for increasing PCB plasmalevels for some congeners.All the cohort studies included in the meta-analysis used anested case-control or case-cohort design (Table 2) and providedAuthors, yearCountryStudy designPeriod of follow-up(years)Type of exposureNo. subjects(cases/controls)OutcomeYoshimura, 2012JapanCohort1968e2007Intoxication (rice oil)1918All cancersLi et al., 2013TaiwanCohort1980e2008Intoxication (rice oil)1803All cancers

Li et al., 2015Japan and TaiwanCohort1968e2008Intoxication (rice oil)3467All cancersGallagher et al., 2011CanadaCa/co2002e2008General population(80/310)CutaneousmalignantmelanomaHardell et al., 2001SwedenCohort (nested Ca/Co)1994e1997General population(82/83)NHLQuintana et al., 2004USACohort (nested Ca/Co)1969e1983General population138/358NHLDe Roos et al., 2005USACa/Co1998e2000General population(97/96)NHLEngel et al., 2007CLUE1USACohort (nested Ca/Co)1970e1980General population74/147NHLEngel et al., 2007JanusUSACohort (nested Ca/Co)1970e1980General population190/190NHLSpinelli et al., 2007CanadaCa/Co2000e2004General population(403/459)NHLCocco et al., 2008France, Germany and SpainCa/CoeGeneral population(174/203)NHLHardell et al., 2009SwedenCa/Co2000e2002General population(97/98)NHLColt et al., 2009USACa/Co1998e2000General population(100/100)NHLLaden et al., 2010USACohort (nested Ca/Co)eGeneral population145/290NHLBertrand et al., 2010USACohort (nested Ca/Co)1982e2003General population205/409NHLViel et al., 2011FranceCa/Co2003e2005General population(34/34)NHLBrEURauner et al., 2012DenmarkCohort (nested Ca/Co)1993e1997General population(239/245)NHLCa/Co  1/4 case-control studies.

ORs as measures of association. Most studies provided estimatescontrolled for possible confounders including demographic andsocio-economic factors, alcohol drinking, tobacco smoking, BMI,family history of NHL and others. Some studies also provided ORestimates for the main PCB congeners, and for subgroups of con-geners according to functional groups (Cocco et al., 2008) or level ofchlorination (Hardell et al., 2009).Two of the 7 cohort studies and four of the 6 case-controlstudies reported a statistically significant trend of increasing riskof NHL with increasing PCB concentration, or an increased risk forlevels higher than, compared to lower than, the median of PCBdistribution (Engel et al., 2007; Spinelli et al., 2007; Colt et al., 2009;Hardell et al., 2009; Bertrand et al., 2010; Viel et al., 2011). A meta-analysis of these studies was performed comparing the highest vslowest quantile or higher vs lower than the median of PCB distri-bution, based on all the studies apart from those by Viel et al. (2011)and by Colt et al. (2009) because they reported PCB serum levelonly as a continuous variable. A summary OR  1/4 1.5 (95% CI: 1.1e1.7)was found, with no evidence of heterogeneity (I2  1/4 22.5%, p > 0.1)and publication bias, and similar results in both cohort and case-control studies (Zani et al., 2013). A meta-analysis of the immu-notoxic PCB congeners 138, 153 and 180, based on 6-8 cohort andcase-control studies reporting suitable data, provided resultsconsistent with the main analysis (Zani et al., 2013).Furthermore, we analyzed the dose-response relationship be-tween total PCB serum levels and OR of NHL on the basis of the ORsreported for each quantile of PCB serum levels, using the medianvalues for each quantile for studies showing ORs for at least threecategories of PCB exposure. Seven of the 13 studies were excludedfrom this analysis: three reported PCB concentrations in subcu-taneous adipose tissue, which were higher than blood levels(Hardell et al., 2001; Quintana et al., 2004; BrEURauner et al., 2012), twoprovided ORs for only two categories of PCB levels (above andbelow the median) (Hardell et al., 2009; Viel et al., 2011), two didnot provide ORs for total PCBs (De Roos et al., 2005; Colt et al.,2009). The findings from the remaining six studies (Engel et al.,2007; two cohorts; Spinelli et al., 2007; Cocco et al., 2008;

Bertrand et al., 2010; Laden et al., 2010) are shown in Fig. 3. Apartfrom one study with extremely high ORs for relatively low PCBvalues (Engel et al., 2007; CLUE1 study), the others showed no ORincrease until PCB serum levels of 1000 ng/g lipid. A meta-analysisof these studies provided an overall OR  1/4 1.42 (95% CI: 1.10e1.83)for PCB serum levels around 1000 ng/g lipid, compared to valueslower than, or next to, 500 ng/g lipid, with low heterogeneityamong the studies (I2  1/4 9.6%, p  1/4 0.4). Too few data were availablefor calculating ORs for PCB serum levels above 1000 ng/g lipid.The Yucheng and Yusho mortality studies on patients acciden-tally exposed to high doses of PCBs and PCDFs showed contrastingresults (Yoshimura, 2012; Li et al., 2013, 2015). Among Yuchengpatients, the SMR for cancer of lymphatic and haematopoietic tis-sue was increased in males (SMR  1/4 3.0, based on 5 deathsobserved) but not in females (no death observed vs 1.6 expected);in the Yusho cohort no excess in haematopoietic malignancies wasfound. However, the neoplasms of lymphatic and haematopoietictissue in the Yucheng cohort included also leukemias, and noseparate data for NHL were available (Yoshimura, 2012; Li et al.,2013).Ameta-analysisofbothcohortsdidnotreportanincreased risk for neoplasms of the lymphatic and haematopoietictissue (Li et al., 2015).4. DiscussionOur systematic review and meta-analysis of the associationbetween PCB exposure and melanoma and NHL showed contrast-ing results when considering a) cohort studies on subjects profes-sionally exposed to PCBs, b) cohort and case-control studies onunexposed people with individual measures of exposure, and c)cohort studies on people undergoing mass poisoning by PCBs andPCDFs.4.1. Cutaneous melanomaThe meta-analysis of the occupational cohort studies showed asummary SMR of 1.32, suggesting a moderate increase of the risk

for occupational exposure. However, the dose-response analysisprovided inconsistent results, with only two studies showing anincrease of the risk of melanoma with increasing duration ofemployment or cumulative exposure. It is noteworthy that thelargest cohort study carried out among capacitor manufactureworkers, including a pooled analysis of three cohorts, some ofwhich had been highly exposed to PCBs, showed the lowest risk forthe highest level of exposure (Ruder et al., 2014), estimated byvalidated semi-quantitative job-exposure matrices (Hopf et al.,2014a, 2014b). Furthermore, a recent meta-analysis on occupa-tional exposure to PCBs and risk of melanoma does not support thehypothesis of an association between exposure to PCBs and inci-dence of, or mortality from, malignant melanoma (Boffetta et al.,2016).One only case-control study on cutaneous melanoma and PCBexposure using PCB serum levels as individual measures of expo-sure was carried out so far (Gallagher et al., 2011). The study byGallagher et al. (2011) showed an increased risk of cutaneousmelanoma for PCB exposure, with a dose-response trend. However,it is relatively small (80 cases) and although it took account for sunexposure and other potential confounders, suffers from somedrawbacks: the cases and controls came from two previous,different studies, with only an approximate age and sex matching;the retrospective design of the research, with PCB serum levelsmeasured in melanoma cases after the beginning of the disease;and finally, more importantly, the fact that PCB analysis was per-formed on frozen serum samples some years after having collectedthem, with longer interval time between collection and laboratoryassay for cases than controls (4-8 and 1e5 years, respectively). Infact, storage of serum in plastic containers has been found to in-crease total PCB concentrations (Karmaus and Riebow, 2004),raising suspicion that higher PCB levels in sera of cases than con-trols may be due, partly, to longer interval between collection andlaboratory analysis.Both Yusho and Yucheng cohort studies on people intoxicatedby rice oil containing PCBs and PCDFs found no excess of deaths forskin cancer (Yoshimura, 2012; Li et al., 2013, 2015). Likewise, noexcess of skin melanoma was observed in the population living in

Michalovce, eastern Slovakia, an area highly polluted by a PCB-producing factory, with geometric means of total PCB serumlevels of 3327.6 and 2751.8 ng/g lipid, in male and female adults,respectively, almost three-fold higher than a control populationliving in an unpolluted area of the same region (Pavuk et al., 2004).In a caseecontrol study from nine European countries, Behrenset al. (2010) investigated the association between uveal melanomaand exposure to PCBs, which was assigned if subjects reportedoccupational exposure to transformer/capacitor oils at personalinterviews. Analyses were adjusted for age, country, eye color, andhistory of ocular damage from ultraviolet light, though no infor-mation was available on lifetime sun exposure. Only men reportedexposure to transformer/capacitor oils. In men, the OR for anyexposure was 2.74 (99.3% CI, 1.07e7.02). However, a re-analysis ofthe same study using job-specific questionnaires to solicit detailedquestions on occupational tasks, which have higher specificity onthe assessment of job-related exposures as compared to the othertools, found no statistically significant increased risk of uvealmelanoma for exposure to PCBs (OR  1/4 1.23, 95% CI: 0.55e2.74)(Behrens et al., 2012).A recent review (Fortes and De Vries, 2008) suggested a role forsome specific work-related exposures in aetiology of cutaneousmelanoma. However, they included in the analysis also studies inwhich the subjects had been exposed to different chemicals (sol-vent, PAH, pesticides, etc.), thus not allowing to evaluate the as-sociation between exposure to PCBs and risk for melanomaproperly.4.2. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma

None of occupational cohort studies showed statistically sig-nificant excess of NHL or all lymphomas together, with a summarySMR  1/4 0.94. Accordingly, analysis by duration of employment orcumulative exposure to PCBs provided no evidence of association.Among the 11 cohort and case-controls studies with individual PCBmeasures, only one showed a statistically significant increased riskof NHL. However, a meta-analysis of these studies provided anoverall statistically significant OR  1/4 1.40 (Zani et al., 2013). Thedose-response analysis based on six studies showed an increasedrisk of NHL for PCB serum levels around 1000 ng/g lipid, comparedto lower than or next to 500 ng/g lipid, with an overall OR  1/4 1.42,with low heterogeneity among the studies. No data were availablefor evaluation of NHL for higher PCB serum levels. Although reversecausality may theoretically bias the results of case-control studiestoward positive findings, as serum samples were collected after thedisease occurrence, this is unlikely to have occurred in most case-control studies on melanoma or NHL and PCB, because incidencecases were enrolled before starting chemotherapy. Anyway, themeta-analytic results of cohort studies, in which sera collectionprecedes the disease occurrence, and case-control studies are verysimilar (Zani et al., 2013).

Freeman and Kohles (2012) recently conducted a meta-analysisof six case-control studies on NHL risk according to serum levels of10 PCB congeners, and computed a summary statistically signifi-cant OR  1/4 1.43 for the sum of these congeners (95% CI: 1.31e1.55).Another review (Kramer et al., 2012) concluded that there wassufficient evidence supporting the causal role of PCBs in the genesisof lymphomas, the most important results coming from populationstudies. The commonly hypothesized mechanism for developmentof NHL due to PCB exposure is immune dysregulation able todecrease the resistance of the subject, the control of cell prolifer-ation and differentiation. Indeed, both toxicological and epidemi-ological data showed immunotoxic effects of PCBs, particularlyimmunosuppression, which has been recognized as the mostimportant risk factor for NHL development (Kramer et al., 2012;Strauss and Heiger-Bernays, 2012). However, the Yusho andYucheng cohort studies showed inconsistent results, with an excessof neoplasms of lymphatic and hematopoietic tissue, includingsome leukemia cases, only in males in one cohort (Li et al., 2013,2015; Yoshimura, 2012). Accordingly, no increased incidence ofNHL was found in a population living in a highly PCB polluted areain Slovakia, with around three-fold elevated PCB serum levels inadults compared to unexposed reference population (Pavuk et al.,2004). Finally, a recent case-control study nested within twocohort studies, with measurements of 6 PCB congeners carried outon pre-diagnostic plasma levels, provided negative results for allcongeners, showing no evidence that a higher body burden of PCBsincreased the risk of subsequent NHL diagnosis (Kelly et al., 2017).Furthermore, the positive results of some case-control studieson melanoma and NHL based on PCB serum levels may be due toreserve causality, because the cases' serum samples were collectedafter the occurrence of the disease. Particularly, weight loss due tocancer and/or chemotherapy may lead to increases in PCB serumlevels as chemicals accumulated in body fat are mobilized. How-ever, incidence cases were usually collected in these studies beforethey underwent chemotherapy in most of them.

4.3. A comparison of this review with the IARC Working Groupevaluation and the Boffetta et al. meta-analysisOur systematic review and meta-analysis of the risk of mela-noma and NHL for PCB exposure provided inconsistent results be-tween occupational cohort studies, population-based studies withindividual measures and studies on highly exposed populations,not allowing to draw firm conclusions on these associations. Theseconclusions are somewhat different from both the IARC WorkingGroup evaluation and the systematic review on PCB exposure andmalignant melanoma by Boffetta et al. (2016).The IARC Working Group performed a comprehensive review ofthe overall evidence for carcinogenicity of PCBs, on the basis ofhuman carcinogenicity data, animal carcinogenicity and mecha-nistic data (IARC, 2016; Lauby-Secretan et al., 2016). The WorkingGroup concluded for sufficient evidence for carcinogenicity of PCBs

in humans for malignant melanoma and limited evidence for NHLand breast cancer (Lauby-Secretan et al., 2013; IARC, 2016).However, some methodological issues of the Working Groupevaluation concerning selection and assessment of epidemiologicstudies should be considered.Particularly, the data on malignant melanoma and PCB exposureprovided a substantial support for the Working Group conclusion ofsufficient evidence for PCB carcinogenicity in humans. They weremainly based on the results of 7 occupational cohort studies, 6 ofwhich were positive, as reported in the Summary of the Monograph(IARC, 2016). However, the criteria used for retrieving and selectingthese 7 occupational studies were undefined. On the other hand,we retrieved 11 occupational cohort studies, only four of whichshowed a statistically significant increased risk of skin cancer.Further support comes from the evaluation of exposure-responserelationship:thestudyreportingastatisticallysignificantexposure-response linear trend in electric power workers wasmentioned in the Summary (Loomis et al., 1997). However, thejoined and updated analysis of the three NIOSH cohorts of workersat electrical capacitor manufacturing plants showing the lowestrisk of melanoma for the highest level of estimated exposure(Ruder et al., 2014) was ignored.The Working Group based its PCB evaluation also on the case-control study on skin melanoma by Gallagher et al. (2011) and onthe case-control study on uveal melanoma by Behrens et al. (2010),which showed a positive association with the history of occupa-tional exposure to PCBs. However, a subsequent analysis of theBehrens et al. study using a more specific tool for defining PCBoccupational exposure did not show a significantly increased risk;these findings were not considered in the final evaluation of theWorking Group (Behrens et al., 2012). Also the findings from thetwo Asian cohorts of poisoned subjects with an overall intake ofPCBs 100-10000 times higher than the general population, that didnot report an increased risk for skin cancer and showed inconsis-tent results for NHL (Yoshimura, 2012; Li et al., 2013, 2015) wereignored in the Summary.Finally, it is noteworthy that the conclusions of the IARCWorking Group on NHL took into account "study size and quality",although the criteria for evaluating study size and quality wereundefined.The study of Boffetta et al. (2016) aimed to carry out a meta-analysis of studies on exposure to PCBs and the risk of malig-nant melanoma. They included both occupational cohort studiesand studies carried out in "populations living in areas with re-ported PCB exposure". The occupational cohort studies included inBoffetta et al. (2016) and our meta-analyses are substantially thesame, although Boffetta et al. used the combined analysis of thethree cohorts of capacitor-manufacturing workers exposed to PCBsat plants in Indiana, Massachusetts, and New York (Ruder et al.,2014), whereas we considered the three cohorts separately.Among the population-based studies, Boffetta et al. included anincidence study on the health effects of exposure to PCBs andmetals in a contaminated community in Sweden (Helmfrid et al.,2012), a study on residents in an area in eastern Slovakia withextensive environmental contamination from a PCB productionsite (Michalovce) (Pavuk et al., 2004) and a mortality and cancerincidence study in cohorts of Swedish fishermen and fishermen'swives (Mikoczy and Rylander, 2009). These three studies, how-ever, did not provide data on individual PCB exposure, andtherefore were not included in our review. Because of the lack ofpopulation-based studies on the association between malignantmelanoma and PCB exposure with individual measures of expo-sure, apart from the Gallagher et al. study (2011), we could notperform a meta-analysis of non-occupational studies, thereforeour findings are not properly comparable with those in Boffetta

et al. (2016).In conclusion, these findings do not provide a strong evidencethat PCB exposure can increase the risk of melanoma and NHL inhumans.Disclosure statementThe authors report no conflicts of interest. The author's affilia-tion is as shown on the cover page. The authors have sole re-sponsibility for the writing and content of the paper.
